food_name: Vietnamese Fresh Spring Rolls (Gỏi Cuốn) — 4 Pieces
food_subgroup: Fresh wrapped rolls / rice paper rolls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 covers spring roll as a general category; no specific entry for Vietnamese fresh (unfried) gỏi cuốn exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers spring rolls generically; no distinct code for Vietnamese fresh rice-paper rolls. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Vietnamese fresh spring rolls (gỏi cuốn). No code assigned.

preparation_method: served at room temperature or chilled

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of 4 fresh Vietnamese spring rolls weighs approximately 240 g (about 60 g per roll), as typically served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Vietnamese Fresh Spring Roll (Goi Cuon) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact entry for gỏi cuốn exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for rice paper, cooked shrimp, lean pork, rice vermicelli, lettuce, fresh herbs, and bean sprouts, weighted by typical recipe proportions reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition literature. Per-100g values reflect the assembled roll without dipping sauce. Energy calculated using Atwater factors. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Rice paper rolls (fresh spring rolls) — analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for gỏi cuốn found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being rice vermicelli (GI ~53 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and rice paper (GI ~40–50 range for rice-based wrappers), moderated downward by the presence of protein (shrimp, pork), dietary fibre (vegetables, herbs), and the intact food matrix. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 46.8) / 100 = 18.7. Serving carbohydrate = 19.5 g/100g × 240 g = 46.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg/100g; ~504 mg per 4-roll serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a 4-roll serving contributes approximately 504 mg sodium (~22% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-sodium dipping sauce or limit sauce portions to manage total sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~18.7 per 4-roll serving) driven by rice paper and rice vermicelli
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include gỏi cuốn as a relatively lower-GI snack option (GI ~40), but should monitor portion size as a 4-roll serving delivers approximately 46.8 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~18.7, which may require insulin or medication adjustment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.8 g/100g), lean protein sources, and abundant vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Gỏi cuốn is a heart-friendly snack choice due to its low saturated fat content, lean shrimp and pork protein, and high proportion of fresh vegetables and herbs; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit the hoisin-peanut dipping sauce to control sodium and added sugar intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gỏi Cuốn (Vietnamese Fresh Spring Rolls) are a traditional Vietnamese dish consisting of cooked shrimp, sliced lean pork, rice vermicelli noodles, fresh lettuce, mint, coriander, Thai basil, and bean sprouts tightly wrapped in softened translucent rice paper (bánh tráng). Unlike fried spring rolls, gỏi cuốn are served fresh and uncooked, giving them a light, refreshing character with a chewy-tender texture from the rice paper and a crisp bite from the vegetables. They are typically served with a hoisin-peanut dipping sauce or nước chấm (fish sauce-based dipping sauce). In Singapore, gỏi cuốn are commonly found at Vietnamese restaurants and are popular as a starter or light snack. Nutritionally, a 4-roll serving (~240 g) provides approximately 312 kcal, 18 g protein, 6.7 g fat, and 46.8 g carbohydrate. The dish is relatively low in saturated fat and rich in lean protein and fresh vegetables, making it one of the more nutritionally balanced Vietnamese snack options. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), moderated by the protein and fibre content. Sodium is moderate (~504 mg per serving), largely from the dipping sauce. The dish is not suitable for those with shellfish or peanut allergies. It is dairy-free but not halal due to the pork component.
common_user_queries: How many calories in goi cuon?
What is in Vietnamese spring rolls?

Goi cuon vs fried spring rolls nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact entry for gỏi cuốn was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for individual ingredients (rice paper, shrimp, pork, rice vermicelli, vegetables) weighted by typical recipe proportions consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition literature. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI data from Atkinson et al. (2008) in Diabetes Care. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100g of assembled gỏi cuốn (without dipping sauce) and are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data. Dipping sauce (hoisin-peanut or nước chấm) would add additional sodium, sugar, and calories if included. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; all ingredients are natural whole foods. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients at the composite level. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component data for the composite at this level of estimation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component data for the composite at this level of estimation. 6. GI is estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous rice vermicelli GI data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for gỏi cuốn was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal. It contains shrimp (crustacean allergen) and peanuts (in dipping sauce). 8. Serving size of 240 g (4 rolls × ~60 g each) is based on typical restaurant portion sizes for Vietnamese spring rolls in Singapore as observed from GrabFood listing context.
